records.ety: [F. supplanter, L. supplantare to trip up one's heels, to throw down; sub under + planta the sole of the foot, also, a sucker, slip, sprout. Cf. Plant, n.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Supplanted (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Supplanting.]

defs: To displace and take the place of; to supersede; to remove or displace by stratagem; as, a rival supplants another in the favor of a mistress or a prince.
quotes: Suspecting that the courtier had supplanted the friend.

defs: To overthrow, undermine, or force away, in order to get a substitute in place of.
quotes: You never will supplant the received ideas of God.

defs: To remove (a thing) and replace it with something else.
